summary:
The invention of papyrus as a writing surface, occurring in ancient Egypt around 2700–2500 BC, was one of the most consequential technological developments of the ancient world. Manufactured from the pith of the Cyperus papyrus plant, which grew abundantly in the Nile Delta, papyrus provided a lightweight, relatively durable, and easily transportable writing surface that transformed the practical possibilities of literacy, record-keeping, and communication. Unlike the heavy clay tablets of Mesopotamia, papyrus scrolls could be transported, rolled for storage, and produced in large quantities from a readily available natural resource.

The earliest known papyrus documents date to the Fourth Dynasty of Egypt, around 2560 BC — the Wadi al-Jarf papyri, discovered in 2013, are among the oldest known examples and contain logistical records relating to the construction of the [[event:1708|Great Pyramid of Giza]]. These early documents demonstrate that papyrus was adopted almost immediately as a practical administrative tool, enabling the sophisticated record-keeping that large-scale state projects required. Papyrus was used for everything from royal decrees and religious texts to business contracts and personal letters.

The impact of papyrus on knowledge transmission was profound. The use of papyrus scrolls allowed Egyptian literary, religious, and administrative knowledge to be copied, stored, and transmitted across space and time in ways that clay tablets could not easily achieve. Papyrus became the dominant writing surface of the Mediterranean world, adopted by Greeks and Romans who built upon its Egyptian prototype. The scroll format it popularised remained the standard form of the book until the codex gradually replaced it in the early centuries AD. Egypt's monopoly on papyrus production gave it considerable economic leverage in ancient Mediterranean trade.
